EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland the afternoon of the 14th, with snow squalls reducing visibility below one quarter mile at times. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week, bitterly cold weather and dangerous wind chills were reported across the area.  Low temperatures of 10 to 20 degrees below zero combined with westerly winds to create wind chills colder than minus 25 for several hours.  The morning low at Erie International Airport was minus 17 and wind chills there got as cold as minus 31 degrees.  Conditions gradually improved during the morning hours. Schools were once again closed throughout northwestern Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some of the coldest weather ever occurred over northwestern Pennsylvania during the middle of February.  An area of arctic high pressure moved from Canada to the western Great Lakes on February 15th. Very cold air surged into northwestern Pennsylvania in response to this high and was accompanied by strong north to northwest winds. The combination of sub zero temperatures and winds gusting to as much as 30 mph created very dangerous wind chills. Wind chill readings dipped below minus 25 degrees during the predawn hours of 15th and then continued for four to six hours. By midday, wind chills were back into the negative teens with slow improvement the remainder of the day. Winds finally lessened during the evening hours as the high approached from the northwest. By daybreak on the 16th, the high was centered over the Upper Ohio Valley resulting in clear skies and another frigid night. Lows on the 15th were mainly 5 to 15 degrees below zero but most of the area dipped below minus 10 on the 16th.  Erie tied it's all time coldest temperatures with a low of -18 on the 16th.   The coldest wind chill reported was minus 30 at the Meadville Airport (Crawford County) with minus 29 at Erie International Airport.  Fortunately all of the schools in northern Ohio were closed on the 16th for the Presidents Day Holiday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub zero temperatures and winds gusting to as much as 30 mph caused winds chills to get as cold as minus 30 degrees during the morning of February 15th. Winds lessened during the evening of the 15th allowing low temperatures on the 16th to dip to minus 10 or colder most areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms intensified along a warm front through the early morning hours across central Pennsylvania, producing torrential rain and frequent lightning across Clearfield, Centre, northern Blair and northern Huntingdon counties. Widespread 2-4 inch amounts were observed over a relatively short duration. A very warm/humid and unstable airmass later in the afternoon promoted additional strong to locally severe thunderstorms which produced isolated damaging winds across the lower Susquehanna Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection formed ahead of an approaching frontal system on the afternoon of June 8th, 2015. Discrete rotating supercells formed ahead of the main line of storms, and one such cell produced an EF-0 tornado in Northumberland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty northwest winds circulating around an intensifying low pressure system and approaching high pressure system had the greatest impact across the Poconos in Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 4th. Peak wind gusts reached between 45 mph and 50 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. In the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania, while still windy, most peak wind gusts were 40 mph or less.||Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at Mount Pocono (Monroe County). ||The strong winds followed the passage of a cold front in Pennsylvania during the early morning on the 4th. A low pressure system on the front over southern New England intensified during the day as it moved toward the Canadian Maritimes. This increased the pressure gradient (difference) between it and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Missouri Valley. Winds decreased that afternoon as the high pressure system moved nearer and the low pressure went farther away in the Canadian Maritimes.

A low pressure system moved slowly through the upper Midwest on May 11th. Northwest Pennsylvania was located in the warm sector out ahead of an approaching cold front. A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was issued around 547 pm for portions of northeast Ohio and northwest Pennsylvania as thunderstorms evolved into a line with bowing segments.  The worst of the damage occurred in Millcreek Township where the Emergency Manager surveyed considerable damage around the intersection of Route 5 and Peninsula Drive, at Waldemeer Park, and Presque Isle State Park. Numerous large trees and power lines were downed by severe thunderstorm winds. Several trees fell on trailers and severely damaged them at a mobile home park prompting a gas leak and evacuations of the trailer park. Downed and uprooted trees extended into Downtown Erie. A spotter estimated wind speeds of 65 mph in the city of Erie.  Numerous trees were also downed in Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a downed tree off of State Route 173 in Wayne Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front draped across central Pennsylvania from an occluded area of low pressure over Lake Huron. Decent low-level lapse rates combined with sufficient shear to produce strong thunderstorms across the area. One long-lived cell produced quarter-sized hail near Bald Eagle and a rotating wall cloud south of State College.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Kane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weak disturbances embedded within a broad upper level trough over the central Appalachians produced clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The convective environment was favorable for organized storms as forecast soundings showed strong deep layer shear and veering wind profile with height. The severe storms resulted in numerous reports of wind damage along with marginal hail and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees on Scotland Road near Quarryville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed Pennsylvania the afternoon and evening of the July 9th, 2015, generating showers and thunderstorms. A QLCS associated with an MCV pushed eastward across southern Pennsylvania ahead of this front, and generated a swatch of wind damage that began in southwestern Pennsylvania early in the afternoon and exited southeastern Pennsylvania during the evening. Farther north, several discrete cells exhibited rotation in the vicinity of a warm front that was draped across the middle Susquehanna Valley. Two of these rotating cells produced tornadoes, one in Selinsgrove and the other near Montoursville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees in eastern Franklin County.

A wildfire started in Kidder Township in Carbon County around Noon EDT on the 3rd between the Francis E. Walker Dam and Jack Frost Ski Area.  While fire fighters were making progress, the gusty south winds and low relative humidity levels on the 4th caused a more rapid increase in the wildfire.  Aircraft were used to assist in battling the blaze and over 100 fire fighters were battling the blaze.  The wildfire was extinguished on the 6th, but not before 540 acres were burned.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized thunderstorm complex tracked southeast from the Great Lakes and produced wind damage across north-central Pennsylvania during the pre-dawn and early morning hours. Additional storms developed on the southwest flank of the decaying nocturnal thunderstorm complex and produced wind damage in several counties along Interstate 80. By late afternoon, scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms produced multiple reports of wind damage, marginally severe hail and localized flooding across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. The majority of the wind damage included downed trees and utility wires. A 15-year-old female was killed by falling branches while camping at the Minister Creek Campground in the Allegheny National Forest in extreme southern Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near Blue Ball. The caller said the funnel did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon heating beneath modest westerly flow led to the formation of scattered showers and thunderstorms across southern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of June 18th, 2015. One report of wind damage occurred in Franklin County, with another strong (albeit unverified) storm affecting Adams and York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Rouzerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Bill pushed up the Ohio Valley and across southern Pennsylvania on June 20th, 2015. A line of thunderstorms pushed across the Laurel Highlands during the afternoon, with several discrete rotating cells developing ahead of the line over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The event ended with a bow echo pushing across southern Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with winds around 60 mph knocked down numerous trees across southern Cambria County.

Numerous large trees and power lines were downed by severe thunderstorm winds. Several trees fell on trailers and severely damaged them at a mobile home park prompting a gas leak and evacuations of the trailer park. Downed and uprooted trees extended into Downtown Erie. A spotter estimated wind speeds of 65 mph in the city of Erie.  Numerous trees were also downed in Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Millcreek Township Emergency Manager surveyed considerable damage around intersection of Route 5 and Peninsula Drive, at Waldemeer Park, and Presque Isle State Park.  Numerous large trees and power lines were downed by severe thunderstorm winds.  Several trees fell on trailers and severely damaged them at a mobile home park prompting a gas leak and evacuations of the trailer park.  Downed and uprooted trees extended into Downtown Erie. A spotter estimated wind speeds of 65 mph in Erie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Bill pushed up the Ohio Valley and across southern Pennsylvania on June 20th, 2015. A line of thunderstorms pushed across the Laurel Highlands during the afternoon, with several discrete rotating cells developing ahead of the line over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The event ended with a bow echo pushing across southern Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall brought Sugar Run Creek out of its banks.  The Cross Keys Trailer Park in Allegheny Township was evacuated due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized thunderstorm complex tracked southeast from the Great Lakes and produced wind damage across north-central Pennsylvania during the pre-dawn and early morning hours. Additional storms developed on the southwest flank of the decaying nocturnal thunderstorm complex and produced wind damage in several counties along Interstate 80. By late afternoon, scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms produced multiple reports of wind damage, marginally severe hail and localized flooding across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. The majority of the wind damage included downed trees and utility wires. A 15-year-old female was killed by falling branches while camping at the Minister Creek Campground in the Allegheny National Forest in extreme southern Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees across a roadway northwest of Dahoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist airmass was in place across the region when a cold front moved into the area from the north on May 30th.  The frontal boundary stalled over the area with a wave of low pressure moving along the front on May 31st.  Multiple rounds of rainfall occurred on May 30 and 31, eventually leading to flooding in Crawford County.  Areas around Conneaut Lake were inundated with water, causing water to enter some basements and a bridge on State Route 285 was compromised due to water undermining it.  Numerous roads were closed including I-79 south of Meadville.  Conneaut Lake received 1.85 inches of rain on the 30th and another 2.62 inches on the 31st for a 2 day total of 4.56.  Much of the county received between 1.5 and 4 of rain during this time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around Conneaut Lake were inundated with water, causing water to enter some basements and a bridge on State Route 285 was compromised due to water undermining it. Numerous roads were closed including a portion of I-79 south of Meadville. Conneaut Lake received 1.85 inches of rain on the 30th and another 2.62 inches on the 31st for a 2 day total of 4.56. Much of the county received between 1.5 and 4 of rain during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weak disturbances embedded within a broad upper level trough over the central Appalachians produced clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The convective environment was favorable for organized storms as forecast soundings showed strong deep layer shear and veering wind profile with height. The severe storms resulted in numerous reports of wind damage along with marginal hail and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down two large trees near Schuylkill Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of an approaching upper trough on the 18th. With precipitable water values over 1.50 inches...some storms produced heavy rain which caused some flash flooding in Allegheny county, PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported that Washington Avenue is flooded in the 1000 block. Water was reported to be up to the car doors with several vehicles stuck. In addition, the ramps to Interstate 79 on Nobelstown Road were flooded and a small landslide was reported on the I-79 northbound ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms mainly across central and northern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 31st. The thunderstorms caused isolated wind damage as well as bringing some flooding rains to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large tree limbs and wires in Frystown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front helped trigger showers and thunderstorms mainly across central and northern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 31st. The thunderstorms caused isolated wind damage as well as bringing some flooding rains to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large tree limbs and wires in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of overnight showers and thunderstorms caused some typical poor drainage flooding in eastern Pennsylvania as well as flash flooding in Northampton County before dawn on June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding in Bethlehem Township.  One water rescue was performed. Through the early morning, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 2.5 inches in central Northampton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection formed ahead of an approaching frontal system on the afternoon of June 8th, 2015. Discrete rotating supercells formed ahead of the main line of storms, and one such cell produced an EF-0 tornado in Northumberland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (over 4 inches in 2 hours) brought flash flooding to the area.  A family was evacuated from a home in the 600 block of Shippensburg Road (Route 533) in North Newton Township.  Flooding was reported at the Laughlin Mill in Newville.  Flash flooding was also reported in the Orrstown area and in Shippensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Flash flood was observed in the Carlisle area of Cumberland County.  Several water rescues were noted, include one on Lincoln Street.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports of more then 1 foot of water flowed onto the parking lot of businesses at the intersection of Clay, North Bedford and North Hanover Streets in Carlisle.  Two vehicles were stranded in the high waters, and one person was rescued by boat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty northwest winds circulating around an intensifying low pressure system and approaching high pressure system had the greatest impact across the Poconos in Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 4th. Peak wind gusts reached between 45 mph and 50 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs and wires. In the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania, while still windy, most peak wind gusts were 40 mph or less.||Peak wind gusts included 48 mph at Mount Pocono (Monroe County). ||The strong winds followed the passage of a cold front in Pennsylvania during the early morning on the 4th. A low pressure system on the front over southern New England intensified during the day as it moved toward the Canadian Maritimes. This increased the pressure gradient (difference) between it and an approaching high pressure system from the lower Missouri Valley. Winds decreased that afternoon as the high pressure system moved nearer and the low pressure went farther away in the Canadian Maritimes.
A series of overnight showers and thunderstorms caused some typical poor drainage flooding in eastern Pennsylvania as well as flash flooding in Northampton County before dawn on June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavier rain and especially blocked and clogged storm drains led to a roadway closure on the Blue Route (Interstate 476) during the morning commute on the 1st. All the northbound lanes of the interstate were closed from 530 a.m. EDT to 645 a.m. EDT between exit 9 for Broomall and exit 13 for Villanova.  Heavier rain fell at the Philadelphia International Airport between 2 a.m. and 630 a.m. that morning and the event total was 1.09 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms produced isolated wind damage and one report of sub-severe hail (nickel-sized) across central Pennsylvania.  Torrential downpours led to flash flooding across Lebanon and Schuylkill Counties where over 3 of rain fell in a short period of time (from 19-22 UTC).

A warm and very humid airmass led to afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms across central Pennsylvania. A line north-south of storms formed over the Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon. Some of this activity was exhibiting rotation...prompting a tornado warning near Williamsport that went unverified. Later in the evening, another rounds of storms pushed in from western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down two trees in Susquehanna Twp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms intensified along a warm front through the early morning hours across central Pennsylvania, producing torrential rain and frequent lightning across Clearfield, Centre, northern Blair and northern Huntingdon counties. Widespread 2-4 inch amounts were observed over a relatively short duration. A very warm/humid and unstable airmass later in the afternoon promoted additional strong to locally severe thunderstorms which produced isolated damaging winds across the lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down a tree at the intersection of Julia and Front Streets in Sunbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed Pennsylvania the afternoon and evening of the July 9th, 2015, generating showers and thunderstorms. A QLCS associated with an MCV pushed eastward across southern Pennsylvania ahead of this front, and generated a swatch of wind damage that began in southwestern Pennsylvania early in the afternoon and exited southeastern Pennsylvania during the evening. Farther north, several discrete cells exhibited rotation in the vicinity of a warm front that was draped across the middle Susquehanna Valley. Two of these rotating cells produced tornadoes, one in Selinsgrove and the other near Montoursville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees and wires and closed South Spring Garden Street near Boiling Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough helped trigger a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 8th. The strongest (severe) thunderstorms affected parts of Berks County and the western Lehigh Valley.  The most concentrated power outages occurred in southern Lehigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also tore down poles and wires in Mertztown. A mesonet site measured a wind gust of 60 mph in Mertztown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough preceding a cold front combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms in eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 12th.  The severe thunderstorms in the Lehigh Valley caused about 2,000 homes and businesses to lose power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Spring Township on Bainbridge Circle as well as on Avon Avenue and Shakespeare Drive.  One home was damaged as downed trees punctured a hole in the roof.  Another downed tree damaged a vehicle.

Strong to severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds, large hail and a tornado affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the afternoon into the early evening on the 30th. Hardest hit were the Lehigh Valley which experienced a strong microburst and Chester County which experienced a tornado. A state of emergency was declared in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County).  Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 11,000 of its customers lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Hardest hit by power outages included Whitehall and Catasauqua in Lehigh County and Hanover Township east through Lower Nazareth Township in Northampton County.  First Energy reported 4,000 of its customers lost power in Northampton County. Power was not fully restored until the night of July 1st. Heavy rain also caused some flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos.  The most severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary that slowly lifted northeast on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was photographed near Pennsylvania State Route 30 in West Goshen Township. While wind damage did occur in the township, the funnel cloud itself did not reach the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms, particularly during the late afternoon and early evening on the 27th brought some heavy rain to extreme southeast Pennsylvania and caused some poor drainage flooding in urban areas in and around Philadelphia.  Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 2.0 inches. Lighter showers arrived during the morning on the 27th. Precipitation remained fairly light until later in the afternoon. The showers and thunderstorms ended during the middle of the evening on the 27th. The heavy precipitation was caused by an anomalously strong low pressure that moved through the Ohio Valley and the region on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the late afternoon and early evening of the 27th forced the closure of Kelly Drive within Philadelphia. Event precipitation totals included 1.79 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 1.34 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable air mass was present Friday afternoon and evening as a warm front lifted north that morning across the state of Pennsylvania and New York. A shortwave aloft which was embedded within the cyclonic flow interacted with this front and showers and thunderstorms developed over central New York.  Late Friday afternoon the front started to slowly shift southward as a cold front. Showers and thunderstorms continued to develop along the front into the late evening hours as it moved southward into Pennsylvania. These storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced severe winds as it moved across the area. The thunderstorm resulted in numerous trees being knocked over on Orwell Hill road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms increased in coverage during the late afternoon and early evening along a pre-frontal corridor of high dewpoints and moderate instability from eastern KY into northwest PA. Marginally severe storms produced isolated wind damage along the western Alleghenies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees along Eleven Mile Road southeast of Shinglehouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slowly westward moving thunderstorms that formed along a backdoor cold front caused flash flooding in parts of Northampton and Upper Bucks Counties during the evening of the 15th.  Reported event precipitation totals reached 2 to 3 inches. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded 4 inches in south central Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding as well as flash flooding of smaller creeks in central and southern Northampton County. The heaviest rain fell in a northwest to southeast axis across the county from eastern East Allen Township through Lower Nazareth Township, Bethlehem Township, Lower Saucon Township and Williams Township. In Bethlehem, flash flooding forced the closure of Easton Avenue at Willow Park Road.  Flash flooding along the Nancy Run also washed out sections of Willow Park Road south through Walnut Street.  A large sink hole near a Pennsylvania Power and Light transmission tower on Clermont Street forced the evacuation of 25 to 30 families for a day. A home on Garrison Street had damage to its foundation and sidewalk. Wilson Avenue, near the Lehigh River became a river itself. Other roadways near the Lehigh River were washed away.  In Easton, parts of Hope Road washed away. Event precipitation totals included 2.89 inches in Glendon and 2.21 inches in Easton.

Flood waters also reached into the bottom of the historic Tannery and Waterworks. Other parts of the historic downtown and northern neighborhoods also suffered from poor drainage flooding. The Monocacy Creek at the Illicks Mill Road gage reached its 4.5 foot flood stage at 837 p.m. EDT on the 15th and crested at 5.99 feet at 1045 p.m. EDT on the 15th.

Showers and thunderstorms, particularly during the late afternoon and early evening on the 27th brought some heavy rain to extreme southeast Pennsylvania and caused some poor drainage flooding in urban areas in and around Philadelphia.  Event precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 2.0 inches. Lighter showers arrived during the morning on the 27th. Precipitation remained fairly light until later in the afternoon. The showers and thunderstorms ended during the middle of the evening on the 27th. The heavy precipitation was caused by an anomalously strong low pressure that moved through the Ohio Valley and the region on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the late afternoon and early evening of the 27th forced the closure of Colwell Road in Conshohocken. Event precipitation totals included 1.90 inches in Blue Bell, 1.80 inches in Fort Washington and 1.74 inches in Bala Cynwyd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather occurred across southeast Pennsylvania on the 23rd with high temperatures reaching into the lower to mid 90s and afternoon heat indices of around 100F. This air mass provided the necessary energy for a strong to severe squall line of thunderstorms that moved through the state during the late afternoon and early evening. Highest temperatures included 95 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, 94 degrees in Bala Cynwyd (Montgomery County) and Swarthmore (Delaware County) and 93 degrees in Langhorne (Bucks County) and West Chester (Chester County).

A cluster of showers and thunderstorms formed along gust front and sea breeze front boundaries in southeastern Pennsylvania during the late evening of the 26th and had little movement before they dissipated early on the 27th. Consequently, very heavy rain fell along the Montgomery and Chester County border and flash flooding occurred. The heavy rain began between 930 p.m. and 1030 p.m. EDT on the 26th. The rain ended between 1 a.m. and 2 a.m. EDT on the 27th.  Reported event precipitation totals reached 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in and around Collegeville. Roadways were flooded and some flood waters went into the basements of homes. A mesonet site in Collegeville had an event total of 3.81 inches of rain. A mesonet site in nearby Eagleville had an event total of 2.04 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, multiple thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos on the afternoon and evening of the 30th. The flash flooding in the Poconos persisted into July 1st. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two inches in the Lehigh Valley and five inches in the Poconos.||The heavy rain on the 30th culminated a very wet month of June across Eastern Pennsylvania and Pennsylvania as a whole.  The June monthly precipitation total of 7.59 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was the 5th wettest June on record and the 8.88 June monthly precipitation total at the Philadelphia International Airport was the 3rd wettest June on record.  The June precipitation total of 6.22 inches in Reading (Berks County), was 2.44 inches wetter than normal and the June precipitation total of 8.77 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), was 4.39 inches wetter than normal. For the state of Pennsylvania, it was the second wettest June on record (8.20 inches) dating back to 1895.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, torrential downpours caused flash flooding in Coplay Borough. Several roadways were flooded with vehicles trapped in flood waters. A water rescue occurred on Catherine Street.  Doppler radar storm total estimates reached around two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon and evening of August 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree uprooted and several trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough coupled with an unseasonably warm air mass triggered scattered thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon on the 27th. A couple of the thunderstorms were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 52-year-old man was struck by lightning while walking his dog. He was hospitalized, but was expected to survive.

For the state of Pennsylvania, it was the second wettest June on record (8.20 inches) dating back to 1895.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, torrential downpours caused flash flooding in Northampton Borough as the Dry Run flooded.  On Banks Street, about 12 to 14 homes were flooded. Gasoline was also released into the flood waters. Flash flooding extended to East 8th Street where one home suffered structural damage and two people were trapped inside. The family was displaced and assisted by the Red Cross. Doppler radar storm total estimates reached around two inches.

With moderate instability and shear, several storms produced severe wind damage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds, large hail and a tornado affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the afternoon into the early evening on the 30th. Hardest hit were the Lehigh Valley which experienced a strong microburst and Chester County which experienced a tornado. A state of emergency was declared in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County).  Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 11,000 of its customers lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Hardest hit by power outages included Whitehall and Catasauqua in Lehigh County and Hanover Township east through Lower Nazareth Township in Northampton County.  First Energy reported 4,000 of its customers lost power in Northampton County. Power was not fully restored until the night of July 1st. Heavy rain also caused some flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos.  The most severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary that slowly lifted northeast on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down in Honey Brook Township.  No injuries were reported, although several homes as well as numerous trees were damaged.  The tornado touched down near Given Road and moved northeast lifting near Talbotville Road.  Roof panels were torn from one home. A mobile home was unseated from its moorings. Two uninjured people were trapped within the mobile home and had to be rescued by the local fire department. A barn along Talbotville Road had its roof torn off and wall damaged during the storm. Farmstands were damaged along Talbotville Road. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped along the damage path.  Debris was scattered across roadways and farm fields and included pieces of tin roofs as well as insulation.  Several roads were impassable because of downed trees and power lines.  The path length was approximately 0.7 of a mile, the maximum path width was estimated to be 100 yards and the estimated peak wind gust was 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms formed along gust front and sea breeze front boundaries in southeastern Pennsylvania during the late evening of the 26th and had little movement before they dissipated early on the 27th. Consequently, very heavy rain fell along the Montgomery and Chester County border and flash flooding occurred. The heavy rain began between 930 p.m. and 1030 p.m. EDT on the 26th. The rain ended between 1 a.m. and 2 a.m. EDT on the 27th.  Reported event precipitation totals reached 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding in and around Pheonixville.  A mesonet site within Phoenixville had an event total of 3.50 inches of rain.

In addition to the severe weather, multiple thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos on the afternoon and evening of the 30th. The flash flooding in the Poconos persisted into July 1st. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two inches in the Lehigh Valley and five inches in the Poconos.||The heavy rain on the 30th culminated a very wet month of June across Eastern Pennsylvania and Pennsylvania as a whole.  The June monthly precipitation total of 7.59 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was the 5th wettest June on record and the 8.88 June monthly precipitation total at the Philadelphia International Airport was the 3rd wettest June on record.  The June precipitation total of 6.22 inches in Reading (Berks County), was 2.44 inches wetter than normal and the June precipitation total of 8.77 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), was 4.39 inches wetter than normal. For the state of Pennsylvania, it was the second wettest June on record (8.20 inches) dating back to 1895.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams and poor drainage locations in central Carbon County from around Lehighton northeast through parts of Penn Forest and Towamensing Townships to the Monroe County border. Event precipitation totals include 3.57 inches in Lehighton and 3.19 inches in Jim Thorpe and 2.28 inches in Albrightsville. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached nearly 5 inches along the Monroe County border in the Pohopoco Creek drainage basin.

Damage was mainly confined to trees, however one barn sustained roof damage. This barn was along Dogtown Road and was the end of the damage path. Maximum estimated winds were 80 mph, classifying this as an EF0 tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado was confirmed south of Riverside...with multiple trees down and damage to a small barn and a home. A shed was destroyed and a car was damaged by flying debris. Maximum estimated winds were 100 mph, classifying this as an EF1 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado was confirmed in Selinsgrove on July 9, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed that an EF0 tornado touched down on the south side of Selinsgrove near the intersection of Sand Hill Road and South Market Street. The tornado traveled northeastward across the Isle of Que before lifting as it encountered the Susquehanna River near the intersection of East Pine Street and Front Street. There was some tree and crop damage, with some trees being snapped and others uprooted. Maximum wind speed was around 70 mph, classifying this as an EF0 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over the upper Ohio Valley during the day on May 31st ahead of surface low pressure that would cross the region in the evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado was confirmed, with maximum wind speeds of 80mph, after a survey of damage and review of available radar data and security camera footage. The most significant damage occurred near where the tornado began, at the Sheetz gas station at the corner of Route 18 and 518. At this location, part of a station canopy was removed and dispersed downwind to the north. Numerous panels of a wood fence were blown from north to south and the wind was observed on camera footage to lift the rear of a pickup truck, rotating it over 90 degrees before depositing it on it's side. Nearby, many large trees and branches were snapped, with  one tree uprooted and blown toward the northwest. One home experienced loss of shingles over a large portion of it's roof. ||The damage path continued northeastward and consisted primarily of snapped and uprooted trees. Damage became much sparser with time, and seemed to end just north of Sample Road. The path length was just over a mile long and max width was 250 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed Pennsylvania the afternoon and evening of the July 9th, 2015, generating showers and thunderstorms. A QLCS associated with an MCV pushed eastward across southern Pennsylvania ahead of this front, and generated a swatch of wind damage that began in southwestern Pennsylvania early in the afternoon and exited southeastern Pennsylvania during the evening. Farther north, several discrete cells exhibited rotation in the vicinity of a warm front that was draped across the middle Susquehanna Valley. Two of these rotating cells produced tornadoes, one in Selinsgrove and the other near Montoursville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated around 60 mph knocked down trees on Paulson Hollow Road and wires onto a mobile home.

First Energy reported 4,000 of its customers lost power in Northampton County. Power was not fully restored until the night of July 1st. Heavy rain also caused some flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos.  The most severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary that slowly lifted northeast on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud sighted over Whitehall Township did not reach the ground, however a microburst still caused wind damage in the township.

Strong to severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds, large hail and a tornado affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the afternoon into the early evening on the 30th. Hardest hit were the Lehigh Valley which experienced a strong microburst and Chester County which experienced a tornado. A state of emergency was declared in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County).  Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 11,000 of its customers lost power in the Lehigh Valley. First Energy reported 4,000 of its customers lost power in Northampton County. Power was not fully restored until the night of July 1st. Heavy rain also caused some flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos.  The most severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary that slowly lifted northeast on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained Skywarn spotter saw a funnel cloud over Fogelsville.  The funnel cloud did not touch down and cause any wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass caused high temperatures to reach the mid 90s in Philadelphia on Friday the 12th. Combined with relatively high dew points, the peak hourly heat index reached 99 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The heat forced schools to dismiss children early in Philadelphia. Actual high temperatures included 95 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and 93 degrees at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport.  While humid weather continued beyond the 12th, a weak back door cold front brought in a slightly cooler air mass into the area on the 13th.
The remnants of tropical System Bill moved across the upper Ohio valley bringing scattered severe thunderstorms and isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported around a foot of water moving across the intersection of Bridge Street and North Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual flash flooding from thunderstorms on June 30th continued overnight into July 1st in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in central Monroe County lasted into the early morning of July 1st. Hardest hit were Chestnuthill and Pocono Townships. The Monroe County Office of Emergency Management urged motorists to stay off the road. In Chestnuthill Township, the Pohopoco Creek flooded near Camp Jaycee. Merwinesburg Road was closed from Pennsylvania State Route 115 to Jonas Road. Ziegler Road was also closed near Jonas Road. In Pocono Township, southbound Pennsylvania State Route 611 along the Pocono Creek and near the intersection with Pennsylvania State Route 715 in Tannersville was flooded. There were mud slides in the area also. Farther south along the same creek, Warner Road (a partial wash-out) and Pennsylvania State Route 611 were also closed because of flooding. In Polk Township, Dotters Corner Road (near Dotters Creek) near Pennsylvania State Route 534 was flooded and closed. Event precipitation totals included 4.63 inches in Pocono Pines, 3.84 inches in Pocono Summit, 2.78 inches in Saylorsburg and 2.71 inches in McMichaels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with heavy rain caused roadway and poor drainage flooding in Monroe County during the evening of the 14th.  The heaviest rain fell between 630 p.m. and 9 p.m. EDT that evening with event totals reaching 1 to 3 inches in most areas in the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor drainage flooding followed the passage of thunderstorms through Monroe County on the evening of the 14th. The heaviest rain fell in the central and western part of the county.  Event precipitation totals included 2.56 inches in McMichaels, 2.47 inches in Pocono Pines, 2.17 inches in Syndersville and 1.78 inches in Pocono Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms that formed along a lee side trough moved across eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 14th. The thunderstorms caused isolated wind and lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike knocked a tree down onto wires in Lower Saucon Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms that formed along a lee side trough moved across eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 14th. The thunderstorms caused isolated wind and lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Franconia Township, knocked down a mailbox and blew it into a neighboring house's driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slowly westward moving thunderstorms that formed along a backdoor cold front caused flash flooding in parts of Northampton and Upper Bucks Counties during the evening of the 15th.  Reported event precipitation totals reached 2 to 3 inches. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded 4 inches in south central Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding as well as flash flooding of smaller creeks in Durham and Nockamixon Townships.  Doppler Radar storm totals reached 3 to 4 inches near the Northampton County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving storm system brought a warm front north across the region...quickly followed by a cold front. While some gusty winds were reported, the only severe weather was a weak EF0 tornado that occurred in the warm sector behind the warm front. Isolated flash flooding also occurred with the training of rain showers ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported several trees down across the area, mainly from saturated soils and wind gusts to near 40 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon and evening of August 3rd.

On June 14, the front moved north over the lake and stalled just north of Erie, PA. Areas to the south were in the warm sector with much of area getting into the low to mid 80s. Moisture remained abundant in the region as precipitable water values and dew points remained high. A shortwave from southern Michigan/northern Indiana moved east and thunderstorms became severe after 5pm in a high moisture and better sheared environment in northwest PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported thunderstorms winds blew a tree down in Franklin Township.

A shortwave from southern Michigan/northern Indiana moved east and thunderstorms became severe after 5pm in a high moisture and better sheared environment in northwest PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported thunderstorm winds brought down a tree and powerlines down near Union City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance aloft and low level instability allowed scattered severe thunderstorms to develop across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported flooded roadways.

Reported event precipitation totals reached 2 to 3 inches. Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded 4 inches in south central Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Residual flooding along the Monocacy Creek in Bethlehem continued into the overnight of the 15th. The Monocacy Creek at the Illicks Mill Road gage was above its 4.5 foot flood stage from 837 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 107 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 5.99 feet at 1045 p.m. EDT on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approaching from the Great Lakes and a weak pre-frontal surface trough helped to trigger a few strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 4th. The area most affected by the thunderstorms extended from Berks County into the Lehigh Valley where damaging wind gusts, power outages, and a lightning strike to a structure were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees near Interstate 78 along the Berks/Lehigh County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the Great Lakes Region Tuesday and the attendant surface low pressure system drifted northeast into central Quebec. This surface low pushed a cold front across the area. Showers and thunderstorms started to develop over the region late Tuesday afternoon and became severe by Tuesday evening as the thunderstorms moved east. These thunderstorms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over the area and produced one inch sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours of June 23rd, a cluster of thunderstorms developed over Lake Erie. These storms moved onshore and caused some damage in Erie and Crawford Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees and large limbs in the Meadville area.

On June 14, the front moved north over the lake and stalled just north of Erie, PA. Areas to the south were in the warm sector with much of area getting into the low to mid 80s. Moisture remained abundant in the region as precipitable water values and dew points remained high. A shortwave from southern Michigan/northern Indiana moved east and thunderstorms became severe after 5pm in a high moisture and better sheared environment in northwest PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported a couple trees blown down from thunderstorm winds near Cochranton.

The combination of gusty northwest winds and an approaching arctic air mass caused wind chill factors in the Poconos to fall to around 20 degrees below zero during the first half of the day on the 31st. The lowest hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono was 22 degrees below zero while the actual low temperature was 2 degrees above zero. The low temperature in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County) was 7 degrees above zero. Across the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania wind chill factors dropped to the single numbers below zero with low temperatures around 10 degrees above zero.

An upper air disturbance and a weakening low pressure system over Western Pennsylania dropped 1 to 2 inches of snow across parts of the western Poconos, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley during the afternoon and early evening on the 21st. Less than an inch of snow fell elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow helped cause slippery traveling conditions, especially on side roads.  Several accidents were reported in southern Chester County. A number of flights in and out of Philadelphia International Airport were cancelled and a ground delay program was in effect.  Some schools dismissed early in Chester, Montgomery and Delaware Counties. A few school districts in Berks County had delayed openings the next morning. | |The snow began falling between 1 p.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST on the 21st and ended between 7 p.m. EST and 9 p.m. EST on the 21st.  Representative snowfall included 2.3 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 2.0 inches in Heidelberg Township (Lehigh County), 1.3 inches in Reading (Berks County), 1.2 inches in Danielsville (Northampton County), 1.1 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 1.0 inch at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 0.9 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 0.8 inches in Atglen (Chester County) and Garnet Valley (Delaware County) and 0.5 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Kennett Square (Chester County).

A winter storm dropped heavy snow in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley and a mixture of heavy snow with some sleet and freezing rain in Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the evening of the 23rd into the morning of the 24th. Less wintry precipitation (a faster switch to rain) occurred progressively closer to Philadelphia. Snowfall averaged 6 to 9 inches from Upper Bucks County northward through the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos; 2 to 5 inches across the remaining Philadelphia suburbs and 1 to 2 inches within Philadelphia.  Ice accumulations were generally just a trace.  At the height of the storm, one inch hourly snowfall rates were common.||The snow caused traveling difficulties as well as postponement of social activities on the 24th.  In Berks County, a weather related crash on U.S. Route 222 in Spring Township near Broadcasting Road closed the roadway for one hour.  Also the snow caused power outages to about 100 Metropolitan Edison customers in the county.  Some snow emergencies were declared in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs.  Northampton County Community College postponed its graduation.  Speed restrictions were also in place on all of the major bridges crossing the Delaware River in and around Philadelphia. Philadelphia International Airport instituted a ground stop program. About 24 flights were cancelled on the day and delays averaged 40 minutes.||Precipitation started as snow on the evening of the 23rd throughout Eastern Pennsylvania from south to north between 8 p.m. EST and Midnight EST.  In the Poconos and Lehigh Valley, the snow fell at its heaviest during the pre-dawn hours on the 24th and ended between 8 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 24th. In the Philadelphia northern suburbs and Berks County, the snow changed to freezing rain (with some sleet) between 3 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 24th. Precipitation ended as the freezing mixture between 7 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST on the 24th. In the immediate Philadelphia area including Chester County, the snow changed to rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 24th and remained rain until it ended around 8 a.m. EST on the 24th.  ||Representative snowfall included 9.0 inches in Coolbaugh Township (Monroe County), 8.0 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County) and Forks Township (Northampton County), 7.9 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 7.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 7.0 inches in West Rockhill Township (Bucks County), Nazareth (Northampton County) and Albrightsville (Carbon County), 6.5 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), 6.3 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in Shillington (Berks County), 5.5 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 5.1 inches in Reading (Berks County) and Lehighton (Carbon County),  4.5 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 4.2 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County), 4.0 inches in Horsham (Montgomery County), 3.7 inches in Spring City (Chester County), 3.0 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware County), 2.5 inches in Fort Washington (Montgomery County), 2.0 inches in Fox Chase (Philadelphia) and Penndel (Bucks County), 1.6 inches in Concord (Delaware County) and 1.0 inch at the Philadelphia International Airport. ||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system which moved along the northern Gulf of Mexico on the evening of the 22nd through the morning of the 23rd. It then started to track to the northeast and was in southeast Alabama on the afternoon of the 23rd and moved through southern Georgia overnight on the 23rd. A second low pressure system formed on its warm front that same night in South Carolina. This would become the primary low pressure system as it too tracked to the northeast.  At 1 a.m. EST on the 24th, it was located near Norfolk, Virginia, 7 a.m. EST on the 24th over the Delmarva coastal waters as it started to intensify rapidly. At 1 p.m. EST on the 24th, the low pressure system was already southeast of Long Island, New York and would pass near Nantucket, Massachusetts later that afternoon.

A quick moving Alberta Clipper, dropping southeast from Canada and through the Northern Plains, tracked across the Ohio Valley and into the Mid Atlantic by the morning of the 6th. Light snow developed across eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning prior to daybreak and continued through the morning commute, before tapering off during the afternoon to early evening. Accumulations were mainly from around one inch to two and a half inches. With temperatures mainly in the 20s and snow to liquid ratios in the 15 to 1 range for most of the area, the snow quickly accumulated and adversely affected the morning commute.  In fact, slippery and icy conditions continued on some area roadways through the evening of the 6th, even after the accumulating snow had ended.  In the West Oak Lane section of Philadelphia, icy conditions along the 6700 block of North Broad Street resulted in a fatal car crash when a 53 year-old man lost control of his vehicle and struck a tree shortly after 900 pm EST in the evening. ||Representative snowfall totals included 2.4 inches in Concord Township (Delaware), 2.3 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware) and Folsom (Delaware) and also Eagleville (Montgomery), 2.2 inches in Wayne (Delaware) and Doylestown (Bucks), 2.1 inches in East Coventry Township (Chester) and Glenmoore (Chester) and also Huffs Church (Berks) and at Blue Marsh Dam (Berks), 2.0 inches in Spring City (Chester) and Kennett Square (Chester) and also West Norriton (Montgomery) and in Gilbertsville (Montgomery), 1.8 inches in Furlong (Bucks) and Collegeville (Montgomery), 1.6 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia) and Schnecksville (Lehigh), 1.5 inches in West Rockhill Township (Bucks) and at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia), and 1.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh).

The combination of strong gusty northwest winds and another arctic air mass over the Poconos produced wind chill factors of around 15 degrees below zero during the evening of the 2nd. Actual air temperatures were in the single numbers above zero.  Winds decreased slowly overnight and air temperatures either held steady or rose slightly toward the morning of the 3rd.  The lowest hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono (Monroe County) was 16 degrees below zero while its actual low temperature was 6 degrees above zero.

A cold front passage accompanied by snow showers occurred during the morning commute in Eastern Pennsylvania.  Snowfall averaged around 1 inch mainly over higher terrain locations in Carbon, Berks and Chester Counties. Less snow fell at locations farther to the east.  Air temperatures were below freezing and untreated roadways were slippery. The snow started before the morning commute started and ceased falling during the commute itself. Representative snowfall included 1.5 inches in Oley (Berks County), 1.0 inches in Lansford (Carbon County) and North Coventry Township (Chester County).

A complex storm system produced snow and a wintry mix across portions of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland from mid morning of the 1st into the early morning hours of the 2nd. A general 1 to 5 inches of snow fell north of I-70 into the evening hours of the 1st, before mixing with and changing to light freezing rain. Precipitation remained snow longer across Mercer county in northwest Pennsylvania, where heavy snow accumulated over 6 inches in 12 hours. Otherwise light freezing rain brought a coating of ice to the ridges of Garrett county, Maryland, and the protected valleys in Tucker and Preston counties in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported more than 6 inches of snow had fallen across parts of Mercer county during the day.

At 1 a.m. EST on the 24th, it was located near Norfolk, Virginia, 7 a.m. EST on the 24th over the Delmarva coastal waters as it started to intensify rapidly. At 1 p.m. EST on the 24th, the low pressure system was already southeast of Long Island, New York and would pass near Nantucket, Massachusetts later that afternoon.

A winter storm dropped heavy snow in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley and a mixture of heavy snow with some sleet and freezing rain in Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the evening of the 23rd into the morning of the 24th. Less wintry precipitation (a faster switch to rain) occurred progressively closer to Philadelphia. Snowfall averaged 6 to 9 inches from Upper Bucks County northward through the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos; 2 to 5 inches across the remaining Philadelphia suburbs and 1 to 2 inches within Philadelphia.  Ice accumulations were generally just a trace.  At the height of the storm, one inch hourly snowfall rates were common.||The snow caused traveling difficulties as well as postponement of social activities on the 24th.  In Berks County, a weather related crash on U.S. Route 222 in Spring Township near Broadcasting Road closed the roadway for one hour.  Also the snow caused power outages to about 100 Metropolitan Edison customers in the county.  Some snow emergencies were declared in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs.  Northampton County Community College postponed its graduation.  Speed restrictions were also in place on all of the major bridges crossing the Delaware River in and around Philadelphia. Philadelphia International Airport instituted a ground stop program. About 24 flights were cancelled on the day and delays averaged 40 minutes.||Precipitation started as snow on the evening of the 23rd throughout Eastern Pennsylvania from south to north between 8 p.m. EST and Midnight EST. Precipitation ended as the freezing mixture between 7 a.m. EST and 8 a.m. EST on the 24th. A cold front passage accompanied by snow showers occurred during the morning commute in Eastern Pennsylvania.  Snowfall averaged around 1 inch mainly over higher terrain locations in Carbon, Berks and Chester Counties. Less snow fell at locations farther to the east.  Air temperatures were below freezing and untreated roadways were slippery. The snow started before the morning commute started and ceased falling during the commute itself. Representative snowfall included 1.5 inches in Oley (Berks County), 1.0 inches in Lansford (Carbon County) and North Coventry Township (Chester County).

An arctic air mass moved across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland on the 24th. Temperatures were well below zero with record lows across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of -20 was recorded at Punxsutawney. A record low of -12 was set at Du Bois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving Alberta Clipper, dropping southeast from Canada and through the Northern Plains, tracked across the Ohio Valley and into the Mid Atlantic by the morning of the 6th. Light snow developed across eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning prior to daybreak and continued through the morning commute, before tapering off during the afternoon to early evening. Accumulations were mainly from around one inch to two and a half inches. With temperatures mainly in the 20s and snow to liquid ratios in the 15 to 1 range for most of the area, the snow quickly accumulated and adversely affected the morning commute.  In fact, slippery and icy conditions continued on some area roadways through the evening of the 6th, even after the accumulating snow had ended.  In the West Oak Lane section of Philadelphia, icy conditions along the 6700 block of North Broad Street resulted in a fatal car crash when a 53 year-old man lost control of his vehicle and struck a tree shortly after 900 pm EST in the evening. ||Representative snowfall totals included 2.4 inches in Concord Township (Delaware), 2.3 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware) and Folsom (Delaware) and also Eagleville (Montgomery), 2.2 inches in Wayne (Delaware) and Doylestown (Bucks), 2.1 inches in East Coventry Township (Chester) and Glenmoore (Chester) and also Huffs Church (Berks) and at Blue Marsh Dam (Berks), 2.0 inches in Spring City (Chester) and Kennett Square (Chester) and also West Norriton (Montgomery) and in Gilbertsville (Montgomery), 1.8 inches in Furlong (Bucks) and Collegeville (Montgomery), 1.6 inches in Rockledge (Philadelphia) and Schnecksville (Lehigh), 1.5 inches in West Rockhill Township (Bucks) and at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia), and 1.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh).

Northwest flow and a crossing upper trough helped to produce lake effect snow bands downwind of Lake Erie during the day on the 7th. Near white-out conditions were encountered in snow squalls which caused a 17-car pileup on Interstate 80 in Clarion County. Two people were killed and several others were injured, closing the interstate for over 6 hours. Snow amounts ranged from 3-5 inches with locally higher amounts reported in Venango county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were killed and several injured when a 17-car pileup occurred due to white-out conditions on Interstate 80 in Clarion County, PA. The two individuals were killed when they got out of their vehicles to help other people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, sleet and/or freezing rain affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania (except for the local Philadelphia area) during the morning and afternoon of the 3rd and persisted into the evening with the greatest impacts in the Poconos. Untreated roadways were hazardous. There were numerous accidents in the Poconos that included an overturned vehicle. Interstate 80 was congested because of the accidents.  Snowfall accumulations were around an inch or less (highest amounts in the Poconos). Ice accumulations were less than one-tenth of an inch in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and one to two tenths of an inch in the Poconos.||Precipitation started as snow on the morning of the 3rd from the Philadelphia northern suburbs north through the Poconos. In the northern Philadelphia suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, the snow changed to sleet around Noon EST on the 3rd.  The sleet then changed to rain by the middle of the afternoon on the 3rd.  In Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, the transition period also included a period of freezing rain. In the Poconos, the snow fell moderately at times during the early afternoon of the 3rd, before precipitation changed to sleet and then freezing rain during the middle and late afternoon. The freezing rain changed to plain rain during the evening of the 3rd. The rain ended in all areas during the early morning of the 4th.  ||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by the combination of a departing strong high pressure over New England on the 3rd which left cold air in place, especially at and near the surface and a low pressure system that developed along a warm front in the Carolinas. This low pressure system tracked to the northeast and brought warmer air near the surface into the area. The low pressure system reached the New Jersey coastal waters on the evening of the 3rd and passed through Long Island and eastern New England overnight.

An Alberta Clipper moved east across the Central Great Lakes on January 6th. A cold front trailing this low swept across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 7th. Snow began around midday on the 6th ahead of the front but transitioned to lake effect snow behind the front. Periods of heavy snow were reported, especially on the higher terrain inland from Lake Erie on the 7th. West to northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph behind the front during the early morning hours of the 7th. The snow and winds combined to create visibilities that were near zero at times. Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times. The snow eventually tapered off during the evening hours. Snowfall totals ranged around 6 inches near Lake Erie to over 10 inches across inland Erie and northern Crawford Counties. A peak of 11.3 inches was reported at Meadville in Crawford County. In Erie County, the peak total was 9.8 inches near Cranesville.  Officially 8.3 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport on the 6th and 7th.

The very persistent and strong northwest winds that started during the day on the 7th caused blowout tide conditions along the tidal Delaware River and its tributaries during the two subsequent low tide cycles during the night of the 7th and morning of the 8th. The lowest tides occurred during the night of the 7th. In Philadelphia, the lowest tide reached 2.23 feet below mean lower low water. In Marcus Hook (Delaware County), the lowest tide was 2.18 feet below mean lower low water.  Blowout tide conditions start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. On the 8th, the northwest winds started to decrease and backed more toward the west. Subsequent low tide cycles did not reach blowout tide conditions.

A persistent and strong northwest flow that started following a secondary cold frontal passage during the late afternoon on the 9th caused blowout tide conditions during the daytime low tide cycle on the 10th along the tidal Delaware River and its tributaries.  At Marcus Hook (Delaware County), the lowest tide reached 2.15 feet below mean lower low water. In Philadelphia, the lowest tide reached 2.29 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tide conditions start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. As the day progressed on the 10th, the northwest winds started to decrease and backed more toward the west. Subsequent low tide cycles did not reach blowout tide conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation, primarily freezing rain, affected all of Eastern Pennsylvania from the late night of the 11th into the morning of the 12th. Snow and sleet accumulations were only a couple of tenths of an inch and mainly over the higher terrain, while ice accumulations reached as high as around one tenth of an inch in the northern Philadelphia suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley.  Because most of the wintry precipitation fell at night on the heels of an unseasonably cold air mass, untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous. Most of the main thoroughfares were treated and the relative number of accidents was low.  However, accidents were reported along Interstate 76 (Schuylkill Expressway and Pennsylvania Turnpike) in the Philadelphia area. Speed restrictions were in place on the Pennsylvania Turnpike as well as the interstates in the Poconos.  Some schools in the Lehigh Valley were closed. Other schools in Eastern Pennsylvania had delayed openings. ||Precipitation started as sleet around Midnight EST on the 12th, except as snow shortly after Midnight EST in the Poconos. In the Poconos, the snow changed to sleet by 4 a.m. EST on the 12th, freezing rain by around 6 a.m. EST on the 12th and rain by 9 a.m. EST on the 12th.  In and around the immediate Philadelphia area, precipitation was mixed with rain from the onset and became just plain rain after 7 a.m. EST on the 12th. In the Philadelphia northern suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley the sleet changed to freezing around 3 a.m. EST on the 12th and then changed to plain rain during the morning of the 12th. The rain ended during the afternoon of the 12th.||Snow accumulations included 0.2 of an inch in Bowmanstown and Lehighton in Carbon County. Ice accumulations included 0.1 of an inch in Martins Creek (Northampton County), West Rockhill Township (Bucks County), Kutztown (Berks County) and Exton (Chester County), 0.07 inches in Reading (Berks County), 0.05 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and Spring Mount (Montgomery County) and 0.03 inches in Wayne (Delaware County).||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by the combination of a low pressure system that moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley and an approaching cold front from the Great Lakes. A retreating arctic air mass left enough cold air in place for precipitation to fall as a wintry mix until it was eroded during the morning of the 12th. Precipitation ended as the cold front approached Eastern Pennsylvania that afternoon.

A wintry mix of snow, sleet and/or freezing rain affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania (except for the local Philadelphia area) during the morning and afternoon of the 3rd and persisted into the evening with the greatest impacts in the Poconos. Untreated roadways were hazardous. There were numerous accidents in the Poconos that included an overturned vehicle. Interstate 80 was congested because of the accidents.  Snowfall accumulations were around an inch or less (highest amounts in the Poconos). Ice accumulations were less than one-tenth of an inch in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and one to two tenths of an inch in the Poconos.||Precipitation started as snow on the morning of the 3rd from the Philadelphia northern suburbs north through the Poconos. In the northern Philadelphia suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, the snow changed to sleet around Noon EST on the 3rd.  The sleet then changed to rain by the middle of the afternoon on the 3rd.  In Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, the transition period also included a period of freezing rain. In the Poconos, the snow fell moderately at times during the early afternoon of the 3rd, before precipitation changed to sleet and then freezing rain during the middle and late afternoon. The freezing rain changed to plain rain during the evening of the 3rd. The rain ended in all areas during the early morning of the 4th.  ||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by the combination of a departing strong high pressure over New England on the 3rd which left cold air in place, especially at and near the surface and a low pressure system that developed along a warm front in the Carolinas. This low pressure system tracked to the northeast and brought warmer air near the surface into the area. The low pressure system reached the New Jersey coastal waters on the evening of the 3rd and passed through Long Island and eastern New England overnight.

Blustery northwest winds around an approaching arctic high pressure system produced wind chill factors of around 15 degrees below zero during the morning of the 10th in the Poconos. The lowest hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono (Monroe County) was 17 degrees below zero with a low temperature of 2 degrees above zero.

Scattered thunderstorms increased in coverage during the late afternoon and early evening along a pre-frontal corridor of high dewpoints and moderate instability from eastern KY into northwest PA. Marginally severe storms produced isolated wind damage along the western Alleghenies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a 64 mph wind gust and pea-sized hail at Chickaree Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow-moving thunderstorms formed over south-central Pennsylvania during the evening hours in a relatively high-CAPE/low-shear environment ahead of a weakening cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail north of Shippensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow-moving thunderstorms formed over south-central Pennsylvania during the evening hours in a relatively high-CAPE/low-shear environment ahead of a weakening cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced winds around 60 mph, knocking down trees at the intersection of Magaro Road and Humer Street in Enola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm brought torrential rains and a report of an earth slide near Shawville, in Clearfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain brought flash flooding and an earth slide near the intersection of Stiner Road and Shawville Croft Highway, Route 879.  Both lanes of the road was closed for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland the afternoon of the 14th, with snow squalls reducing visibility below one quarter mile at times. Wind gusts over 40 MPH occurred with the snow squalls, and thunder-snow was reported. Behind the front from the morning of the 15th into the 16th, temperatures dropped below zero, with extreme wind chills. The lowest wind chills reported were -37 degrees in Canaan Heights, WV, -33 near Strattanville, PA, -32 at Deep Creek Lake, MD, and -24 at East Palestine, OH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill of -24 was recorded at the Pittsburgh International Airport the morning of the 15th, with a wind chill of -26 reported in Wexford. A low temperature of -11 was recorded at Heidelberg the morning of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold frontal passage caused isolated pockets of strong winds in Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening and overnight on the 4th. Most peak wind gusts were between 40 and 45 mph, but reached closer to 50 mph in isolated areas. The strongest winds tossed around unsecured items as well as knocking down weak tree limbs. In Bucks County, in Durham Township, patio furniture was tossed against a building. Peak wind gusts included 49 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 45 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 44 mph at Lenhartsville (Berks County) and Palmer Township (Northampton County), 42 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph in Reading (Berks County).

A wintry mix of precipitation, primarily freezing rain, affected all of Eastern Pennsylvania from the late night of the 11th into the morning of the 12th. Snow and sleet accumulations were only a couple of tenths of an inch and mainly over the higher terrain, while ice accumulations reached as high as around one tenth of an inch in the northern Philadelphia suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley.  Because most of the wintry precipitation fell at night on the heels of an unseasonably cold air mass, untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous. Most of the main thoroughfares were treated and the relative number of accidents was low.  However, accidents were reported along Interstate 76 (Schuylkill Expressway and Pennsylvania Turnpike) in the Philadelphia area. Speed restrictions were in place on the Pennsylvania Turnpike as well as the interstates in the Poconos.  Some schools in the Lehigh Valley were closed. Other schools in Eastern Pennsylvania had delayed openings. ||Precipitation started as sleet around Midnight EST on the 12th, except as snow shortly after Midnight EST in the Poconos. In the Poconos, the snow changed to sleet by 4 a.m. EST on the 12th, freezing rain by around 6 a.m. EST on the 12th and rain by 9 a.m. EST on the 12th.  In and around the immediate Philadelphia area, precipitation was mixed with rain from the onset and became just plain rain after 7 a.m. EST on the 12th. In the Philadelphia northern suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley the sleet changed to freezing around 3 a.m. EST on the 12th and then changed to plain rain during the morning of the 12th. The rain ended during the afternoon of the 12th.||Snow accumulations included 0.2 of an inch in Bowmanstown and Lehighton in Carbon County. Ice accumulations included 0.1 of an inch in Martins Creek (Northampton County), West Rockhill Township (Bucks County), Kutztown (Berks County) and Exton (Chester County), 0.07 inches in Reading (Berks County), 0.05 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and Spring Mount (Montgomery County) and 0.03 inches in Wayne (Delaware County).||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by the combination of a low pressure system that moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley and an approaching cold front from the Great Lakes. A retreating arctic air mass left enough cold air in place for precipitation to fall as a wintry mix until it was eroded during the morning of the 12th. Precipitation ended as the cold front approached Eastern Pennsylvania that afternoon.
