EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As deepening low pressure approached the upper Ohio Valley, strong southeast winds were mixed down on the west side of the Laurel and Chestnut ridges. Wind damage was limited to Westmoreland county, where 20 to 30 reports of trees and wires down were received, as well as roof damage to a mobile home. A 46 MPH wind gust was observed at Arnold Palmer Regional Airport in Latrobe.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along a passing cold front attendant with a large upper level trough located over the border of Ontario and Quebec. The cold front slowly drifted south Tuesday morning over northeast Pennsylvania. Some of the showers and thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter sized hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front coupled with a warm, moist airmass triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania and into central and southern New Jersey during the late evening of the 20th through the early morning of the 21st. The thunderstorms were accompanied by heavy rainfall, leading to areas of flash flooding. Doppler radar estimates, as well as actual storm total measurements, ranged from around 2 to 4 inches where the flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and smaller streams in southern Bucks County. Event precipitation totals included 2.61 inches in Penndel, 2.57 inches in Langhorne, 2.55 inches in Richboro, 2.42 inches in Bensalem, and 2.32 inches in Warminster. Much of this rain fell within a very short time period of about one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front coupled with a warm, moist airmass triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania and into central and southern New Jersey during the late evening of the 20th through the early morning of the 21st. The thunderstorms were accompanied by heavy rainfall, leading to areas of flash flooding. Doppler radar estimates, as well as actual storm total measurements, ranged from around 2 to 4 inches where the flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and smaller streams in lower Montgomery County. Localized flooding on area roadways was reported in Abington Township. Event precipitation totals included 2.87 inches in North Wales, 2.75 inches in Kulpsville, 2.61 inches in Glenside, and 2.54 inches in Hatfield. Much of this rain fell within a very short time period, including 2.30 inches of the reported rainfall at North Wales falling in just 50 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front coupled with a warm, moist airmass triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms across southeastern Pennsylvania and into central and southern New Jersey during the late evening of the 20th through the early morning of the 21st. The thunderstorms were accompanied by heavy rainfall, leading to areas of flash flooding. Doppler radar estimates, as well as actual storm total measurements, ranged from around 2 to 4 inches where the flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from earlier rainfall during the early morning of the 21st caused brief flooding along the Pennypack Creek at Philadelphia. The Pennypack Creek at Philadelphia was above its 7.0 foot flood stage from 5:02 a.m. EDT through 7:06 a.m. EDT on the 21st, and it crested at 7.36 feet at 5:15 a.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms developed over the higher terrain of southwestern Pennsylvania, moving east and producing isolated hail and wind damage in and just east of the Laurel Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Rainsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a wavy frontal boundary across parts of eastern Ohio and much of southwest Pennsylvania the afternoon of the 14th into the early morning of the 15th. Some of these storms were severe, but most produced very heavy rainfall, with local reports of near 4 inches of rain for the event. This brought flash flooding to portions of southwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported flash flooding with Willett Road and Streets Run Road closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough preceding a cold front combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms in eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 12th.  The severe thunderstorms in the Lehigh Valley caused about 2,000 homes and businesses to lose power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm toppled about a dozen tractor tailers at the Osram Sylvania Warehouse on Brodhead Road in Bethlehem.  A couple of snapped trees also rolled into vehicles. The roof of a Crayola warehouse was also damaged.  Numerous trees were knocked down in Bethlehem City and Bethlehem Township. A wind gust of 56 mph was measured at nearby Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak shortwave moving across the region in southwest flow brought showers and thunderstorms, some of which were severe to western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree was reported down on the corner of Mansfield and Holiday Drive on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and a few thunderstorms developed in the warm, moist, airmass out ahead of a surface boundary late on the 19th. A few reports of downed trees were reported, mainly where the strongest cells were located over Venango and Forest counties in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of highways reported trees down on state route 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was draped from west to east just north of Pennsylvania the morning of June 12th, 2015. This boundary began slipping southward as a cold front during the afternoon...ahead of which scattered strong to severe t-storms developed. Resultant damage was due almost entirely to strong winds...with little in the way of hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and power lines near Clearfield with power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms were widespread across Western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia on the 23rd, ahead of a slow moving cold front. Some thunderstorms were severe, with isolated flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a tree down blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance aloft and low level instability allowed scattered severe thunderstorms to develop across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call center reported flooded roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance aloft and low level instability allowed scattered severe thunderstorms to develop across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fire Department reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front and an associated upper level shortwave produced scattered thunderstorms the afternoon and evening of the 10th. A weak and short lived EF-0 tornado occurred in northern Butler county in Pennsylvania, with isolated downburst damage to trees in Muskingum county in Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found a EF-0 tornado, with maximum winds of 80 MPH. The maximum path width was 60 yards. The tornado touched down in a field southwest of West Sunbury just off state route 308, and traveled northeast taking off a roof at a service station. The storm then moved a carport adjacent to the service station 40 yards, and crossed over state route 308 and destroyed a deck on a house. The storm traveled northeast and uprooted large trees before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a pre-frontal trough that was pivoting across the region. With modest instability and shear, several storms produced damaging winds, bringing trees down across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local emergency operations center reported multiple trees down around Cranberry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was draped from west to east just north of Pennsylvania the morning of June 12th, 2015. This boundary began slipping southward as a cold front during the afternoon...ahead of which scattered strong to severe t-storms developed. Resultant damage was due almost entirely to strong winds...with little in the way of hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees across roadways near Coudersport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Bill pushed up the Ohio Valley and across southern Pennsylvania on June 20th, 2015. A line of thunderstorms pushed across the Laurel Highlands during the afternoon, with several discrete rotating cells developing ahead of the line over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The event ended with a bow echo pushing across southern Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing winds around 60 mph knocked down a tree on County Line Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized thunderstorm complex tracked southeast from the Great Lakes and produced wind damage across north-central Pennsylvania during the pre-dawn and early morning hours. Additional storms developed on the southwest flank of the decaying nocturnal thunderstorm complex and produced wind damage in several counties along Interstate 80. By late afternoon, scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms produced multiple reports of wind damage, marginally severe hail and localized flooding across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. The majority of the wind damage included downed trees and utility wires. A 15-year-old female was killed by falling branches while camping at the Minister Creek Campground in the Allegheny National Forest in extreme southern Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced nickel-sized hail near Etters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was draped from west to east across central Pennsylvania...and daytime heating allowed scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop. A few of the storms became severe...with wind damage being the primary result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree near Fisher's Ferry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated clusters of storms developed across northwest PA during the late afternoon and persisted into the evening hours. The strongest storms were located over north-central PA, where some marginal supercell structures were observed. These severe thunderstorms produced several reports of wind damage with trees down across Warren, McKean, Elk, Cameron and Sullivan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds near 60 mph knocked down multiple trees in Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms were widespread across Western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia on the 23rd, ahead of a slow moving cold front. Some thunderstorms were severe, with isolated flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms moved through northwest Pennsylvania ahead of an approaching cold front on the evening of July 19, 2015.  These thunderstorms brought down numerous trees, mainly in eastern Crawford County and extreme southeast Erie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported large tree limbs brought down by thunderstorm winds south of Union City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across central Pennsylvania as an area of low pressure traveled along a stationary frontal boundary that was draped across the area.  A flash flood occurred in Schuylkill County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Bachmanville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front the afternoon of the 18th, with localized flash flooding also occurring into the early morning of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a large tree on a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a wavy frontal boundary across parts of eastern Ohio and much of southwest Pennsylvania the afternoon of the 14th into the early morning of the 15th. Some of these storms were severe, but most produced very heavy rainfall, with local reports of near 4 inches of rain for the event. This brought flash flooding to portions of southwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 991 Call Center reported trees and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a passing shortwave late on the 13th. The thunderstorms produced heavy rain over Allegheny and Westmoreland counties, with flash flooding reported near Leechburgh and Vandergrift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported that several vehicles were stranded on Route 356 at South Leechburg Hill. In addition, flooding was being reported at Allegheny Ludlum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough coupled with an unseasonably warm air mass triggered scattered thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon on the 27th. A couple of the thunderstorms were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Upper Merion Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of southeastern Berks, northwest Montgomery and in Northampton County.  Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding on the Blue Route (Interstate 476) in Broomall (Marple Township). Event precipitation totals included 1.36 inches in Saint Davids and 1.17 inches in Wallingford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down hundreds and hundreds of trees and caused some structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four people were injured in Philadelphia.  Hardest hit were Philadelphia, Chester and in particular central and western Delaware County.  Delaware County declared a state of emergency and suffered an estimated five million dollars in property damage. Five people were injured. While there were multiple reports of funnel clouds, no confirmed tornadoes occurred. Numerous homes and vehicles were damaged, mainly by fallen trees. Many roads were impassable through the next day because of downed trees. SEPTA had to suspend all of its commuter regional rail service on the 23rd in the Philadelphia Metropolitan area because of debris on the tracks and downed wires. Full service was back on the 25th. Amtrak suspended both Northeast Corridor Service between Philadelphia and Washington D.C. and Keystone service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg. That service was restored on the 24th. PECO Energy reported about 250,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania: close to 100,000 in Delaware County, close 75,000 in Chester County and nearly 50,000 within Philadelphia. Mutual assistance repair crews from Ohio, Kentucky, Maryland, West Virginia and New York helped with repairs. At 6 a.m. EDT on the 24th, about 161,000 customers (half in Delaware County) were still without power. That number dropped to 121,000 on the evening of the 24th, 50,000 on the 25th and 16,000 on the 26th. Full restoration occurred on the 28th. In Chester County, Verizon Wireless cell phone service was also lost on the 23rd and the 24th. Commuters were stuck on a train after power was lost on the 23rd. County courts remained closed on the 24th. In Delaware County,  PPL Park was used to distribute ice, cell phone recharge and showers. County offices re-opened on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were knocked down by a severe thunderstorm in Brecknock Township.

Peak wind gusts were estimated to reach 85 mph. Shingles and siding was ripped from homes as well as numerous homes that were struck by fallen trees. About fifteen homes were condemned in the township. Wind damage from the macroburst continued southeast through Chester City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought a steady rain across much of central Pennsylvania on September 12, 2015. Modest CAPE developed across the Lower Susquehanna Valley, with enough instability to generate a severe thunderstorm that knocked down trees in York County, PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Shrewsbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries from convection earlier in the day helped to spark additional thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening on the 9th ahead of an approaching cold front. Where the boundaries collided, thunderstorms with very heavy rain materialized, with rates approaching 2 inches per hour based on radar estimates and spotter reports. Convection started over Etna and Shaler, in northern Allegheny county and propogated northward toward Monaca, Ambridge, and Aliquippa in Beaver county. Several road closures were reported with several feet of water on roadways in both locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 call center reported trees down in Shaler and O'Hara Township.

However, with little shear to support tilted updrafts, some strong to severe wind gusts were realized at the surface as the storms collapsed on themselves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 reported trees and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado was confirmed near Rohrerstown in East Hempfield Township. The tornado uprooted and snapped several large trees as it skipped along a northwest heading for approximately 2.5 miles. At it's peak intensity with maximum winds exceeding 90 mph, the tornado produced significant structural damage to a warehouse located just south of US Route 30. Local and county emergency management officials estimated the damages in the 3 to 5 million dollar range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in State College confirmed an EF-1 tornado, with peak winds around 95 mph, near Rohrerstown in East Hempfield Township, Lancaster County. The tornado touched down around 12:40 am on September 30, 2015. ||The tornado tracked from southeast to northwest along a 2.5-mile path, with two segments of concentrated damage. The first segment covered an approximate one-half mile stretch from the Limespring Farm across US Route 30 and up a nearby hill-side before lifting. The circulation traveled aloft for about 1 mile with a second touchdown just southeast of Centerville Road on Stonebridge Drive. The tornado then crossed Nolt Road and then dissipated just before reaching Bowman Road.||The initial touchdown occurred on the 2300 block of Noll Road between the Limespring Farm and Rohrerstown Elementary School. The Survey team observed an extensive area of healthy snapped and uprooted trees /1 to 2 feet in diameter/ exhibiting a clear convergent signature. Additional damage at the Limespring Farm property included a destroyed outbuilding shed.||Damage indicators confirmed the tornado reached peak intensity as it impacted an industrial warehouse located on Running Pump Road just south of US Route 30. Winds in excess of 90 mph blew in one of the metal garage doors at a loading bay and lifted off a large section of the warehouse roof, which eventually lead to the|partial collapse of interior walls. The local fire chief estimated over 35 percent loss of the roof, with debris blown and deposited on to US Route 30. A wood pallet skid was projected into the side of the warehouse making a clear dent in the building. Two horizontal beams were twisted and detached on the front side of the warehouse where the winds exited the building. Large sections of metal siding were removed from the warehouse. Insulation from the warehouse was recovered at the Four Seasons golf course. Damage along the second tornado path segment consisted mainly of snapped and uprooted trees. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A run of six consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher in Philadelphia caused the death of a 68-year-old man within the city on the 2nd. The high temperature that day was 93 degrees.  The hottest day during that stretch and the entire year occurred on the 3rd when the high temperature reached 96 degrees.  A high pressure system behind a backdoor cold frontal passage on the 4th briefly ended the run of 90 degree weather over the weekend (on the 5th and 6th) before it returned again from the 7th through the 9th. In fact the latter hot spell resulted in the hottest day of the year (95 degrees) on the 8th in both Allentown (Lehigh County) and Reading (Berks County). The hot start to September culminated in the 2nd warmest September on record in Philadelphia at 74.5 degrees (5.4 degrees above normal) and the 3rd warmest September on record in Allentown  at 69.7 degrees (5.8 degrees above normal).  In Reading (Berks County), the September average temperature of 70.3 degrees was 5.0 degrees warmer than normal and in Mount Pocono the September average temperature of 63.8 degrees was also 5.0 degrees warmer than normal. It was the same for the entire state of Pennsylvania.  The average temperature of 66.5 degrees (4.7 degrees above average) was the 4th warmest September on record dating back to 1895.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain preceding and accompanying a cold front occurred across Berks County, the Poconos and Lehigh Valley during the afternoon and early evening of the 12th. While storm total amounts reached as high as around three inches in northern Lehigh County, the recent dry weather resulted in only ponding of water in poor drainage locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain fell across northern parts of Lehigh County and caused some ponding of water in poor drianage locations. Storm totals included 3.22 inches in Shankweilers (North Whitehall Township), 3.12 inches in Schnecksville (North Whitehall Township) and 2.21 inches in  Slatington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms which rode along a wavy cold frontal boundary brought heavy rain into Eastern Pennsylvania on the 10th. The heaviest rain fell right along the Delaware River in the local Philadelphia area where estimates and actual rainfall amounts exceeded four inches. This caused poor drainage as well as some creek flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the afternoon of the 10th caused ponding of water in poor drainage areas along U.S. Route 422 in Pottstown. Heavy rain also affected other parts of northwestern and southeastern Montgomery County.  Event precipitation totals included 3.05 inches in Collegeville, 2.44 inches in Pottstown, 2.26 inches in Gilbertsville and 2.04 inches in Ambler.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain during the evening of the 10th caused flash flooding and deep ponding of water on roadways and poor drainage areas in and around Eddystone Borough in southeastern Delaware County. The calendar day event precipitation total at nearby Philadelphia International Airport was 4.76 inches.

It crested at 7.18 feet at 215 p.m. EDT.  Lincoln Drive had about a foot of water on the roadway. Event precipitation totals in Philadelphia included 4.77 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 3.56 inches in Port Richmond, 2.72 inches at Temple University, and 2.17 inches in Wyndmoor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front from low pressure over the plains lifted north across the upper Ohio Valley  on September 4th. Showers and thunderstorms develop in response to this front. A few of the stronger storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated to be at least 60 mph downed several trees and large limbs  A couple utility poles were also toppled.  At least one road had to be closed because of blockages.

Several wind damage reports were received from Warren County, with isolated reports elsewhere across Central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires on Route 426.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across region the afternoon and evening of September 3rd ahead of an upper level disturbance moving southeast through the Great Lakes. The airmass was moderately unstable but shear was weak. An isolated severe thunderstorm occurred in Crawford County with a tree brought down by thunderstorm winds in Summit Township and Vernon Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sheriff reported a tree down in both Summit and Vernon Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the Gulf and Mid-Atlantic was pulled northward over the region through the 29th by an upper trough over the Lower Mississippi Valley and a cut-off low off of the Southeastern U.S. coast. This brought a period of heavy rain to the region before a cold front passed on the 30th. While the antecedent conditions were dry, over 3.50 inches of rain over portions of southwestern PA caused localized flooding of roadways and basements. 3.43 inches were reported at the Pittsburgh Airport, which was a new 24 hour record on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3.78 inches of rainfall in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries from convection earlier in the day helped to spark additional thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening on the 9th ahead of an approaching cold front. Where the boundaries collided, thunderstorms with very heavy rain materialized, with rates approaching 2 inches per hour based on radar estimates and spotter reports. Convection started over Etna and Shaler, in northern Allegheny county and propogated northward toward Monaca, Ambridge, and Aliquippa in Beaver county. Several road closures were reported with several feet of water on roadways in both locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1.9 inches in one hour.

In addition to the severe weather, multiple thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos on the afternoon and evening of the 30th. The flash flooding in the Poconos persisted into July 1st. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two inches in the Lehigh Valley and five inches in the Poconos.||The heavy rain on the 30th culminated a very wet month of June across Eastern Pennsylvania and Pennsylvania as a whole.  The June monthly precipitation total of 7.59 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was the 5th wettest June on record and the 8.88 June monthly precipitation total at the Philadelphia International Airport was the 3rd wettest June on record.  The June precipitation total of 6.22 inches in Reading (Berks County), was 2.44 inches wetter than normal and the June precipitation total of 8.77 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), was 4.39 inches wetter than normal. For the state of Pennsylvania, it was the second wettest June on record (8.20 inches) dating back to 1895.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in central Monroe County that lasted into the overnight of the 30th. Hardest hit were Chestnuthill and Pocono Townships. The Monroe County Office of Emergency Management urged motorists to stay off the road. In Chestnuthill Township, the Pohopoco Creek flooded near Camp Jaycee. Merwinesburg Road was closed from Pennsylvania State Route 115 to Jonas Road.  Ziegler Road was also closed near Jonas Road. In Pocono Township, southbound Pennsylvania State Route 611 along the Pocono Creek and near the intersection with Pennsylvania State Route 715 in Tannersville was flooded. There were mud slides in the area also. Farther south along the same creek, Warner Road (a partial wash-out) and Pennsylvania State Route 611 were also closed because of flooding. In Polk Township, Dotters Corner Road (near Dotters Creek) near Pennsylvania State Route 534 was flooded and closed.  Event precipitation totals included 4.63 inches in Pocono Pines, 3.84 inches in Pocono Summit, 2.78 inches in Saylorsburg and 2.71 inches in McMichaels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed with a passing cold front on the afternoon of the 21st. Steep low level lapse rates and modest instability allowed for a few strong to severe storms, with gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported trees down in Lackawannock Township. One tree fell onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist airmass coupled with daytime heating allowed widely scattered thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon to early evening of the 19th. A few thunderstorms dropped heavy rain over parts of southeastern Pennsylvania, including in lower Montgomery County. Event precipitation totals reached between 1 and 2 inches, resulting in some poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor roadway flooding in Cheltenham Township (lower Montgomery County). Event precipitation totals included 1.29 inches in nearby Wyncote.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of southeastern Berks, northwest Montgomery and in Northampton County.  Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in Pottstown. Cars were stalled in high waters. A couple of water rescues were performed on High Street (State Route 4031) and College Drive near the Manatawny Creek was closed. Event precipitation totals included 2.34 inches in Pottstown, 2.33 inches in Collegeville, 2.20 inches in Palm and 2.13 inches in Limerick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow and moderate instability ahead of an approaching cold front helped to spawn showers and thunderstorms, some of which were severe, across the northern West Virginia pan-handle, and western Pennsylvania on the 7th. With dry air present aloft, several of the storms produced damaging wind gusts that brought down trees and power lines. Front slowed as it crossed into Pennsylvania early on the 8th, and with plenty of moisture, heavy rain became a threat overnight. Some flooding was reported in Allegheny county in Shaler and McCandless Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees and wires down at the Giant Eagle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms formed along gust front and sea breeze front boundaries in southeastern Pennsylvania during the late evening of the 26th and had little movement before they dissipated early on the 27th. Consequently, very heavy rain fell along the Montgomery and Chester County border and flash flooding occurred. The heavy rain began between 930 p.m. and 1030 p.m. EDT on the 26th. The rain ended between 1 a.m. and 2 a.m. EDT on the 27th.  Reported event precipitation totals reached 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours also caused flash flooding in southern Montgomery County mainly in Upper Merion Township. In Upper Merion Township, a mud slide caused the closure of Balligomingo Road between Front Street and Portland Road. In the King of Prussia area of the township, numerous roadways were flooded. The Schuylkill Expressway (Interstate 76) was badly flooded with traffic either halted or barely able to get through as the heaviest rain occurred. Event precipitation totals included 2.61 inches in King Of Prussia and 1.98 inches in Bala Cynwyd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of the 15th. While the primary impact was flash flooding, some isolated wind and lightning damage also occurred. PECO Energy reported about 3,000 of its customers in Chester, Montgomery, Delaware and Bucks County lost power. The adverse weather also caused a ground stop at Philadelphia International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down large tree limbs in central Delaware County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front over the Finger Lakes region of New York State helped trigger a cluster of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain that moved over the Poconos mainly during the evening of the 26th. The heaviest rain fell over eastern parts of Carbon County and southwestern parts of Monroe County.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 5 inches in eastern Carbon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams and roadways in Penn Forest and Kidder Townships.  Roadway flooding was reported in Penn Forest Township. Precipitation event totals included 3.36 inches in Albrightsville, 3.22 inches in Meckesville and 2.76 inches in Christmans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough helped trigger a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 8th. The strongest (severe) thunderstorms affected parts of Berks County and the western Lehigh Valley.  The most concentrated power outages occurred in southern Lehigh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tore down large tree limbs and wires across the northern tier of Berks County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down hundreds and hundreds of trees and caused some structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four people were injured in Philadelphia.  Hardest hit were Philadelphia, Chester and in particular central and western Delaware County.  Delaware County declared a state of emergency and suffered an estimated five million dollars in property damage. Five people were injured. While there were multiple reports of funnel clouds, no confirmed tornadoes occurred. Numerous homes and vehicles were damaged, mainly by fallen trees. Many roads were impassable through the next day because of downed trees. SEPTA had to suspend all of its commuter regional rail service on the 23rd in the Philadelphia Metropolitan area because of debris on the tracks and downed wires. Full service was back on the 25th. Amtrak suspended both Northeast Corridor Service between Philadelphia and Washington D.C. and Keystone service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg. That service was restored on the 24th. PECO Energy reported about 250,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania: close to 100,000 in Delaware County, close 75,000 in Chester County and nearly 50,000 within Philadelphia. Mutual assistance repair crews from Ohio, Kentucky, Maryland, West Virginia and New York helped with repairs. At 6 a.m. EDT on the 24th, about 161,000 customers (half in Delaware County) were still without power. That number dropped to 121,000 on the evening of the 24th, 50,000 on the 25th and 16,000 on the 26th. Full restoration occurred on the 28th. In Chester County, Verizon Wireless cell phone service was also lost on the 23rd and the 24th. Commuters were stuck on a train after power was lost on the 23rd. County courts remained closed on the 24th. In Delaware County,  PPL Park was used to distribute ice, cell phone recharge and showers. County offices re-opened on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees along Sullivan Trail in Pocono Township.

In Plainfield Township, both Sullivan Trail and Bangor Road were closed. Event precipitation totals included 3.18 inches in Lower Nazareth Township and  2.29 inches in Nazareth. In Plainfield Township, the two day precipitation total was 4.69 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of southeastern Berks, northwest Montgomery and in Northampton County.  Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Amity Gardens section of Amity Township.  Event precipitation totals included 2.65 inches in Boyertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front developed a line of strong thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 30th in Eastern Pennsylvania.   This line of thunderstorms dropped around an inch to inch and a half of rain in one hour in parts of Berks County and the Lehigh Valley. This caused isolated urban area flash flooding as well as general poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused urban flash flooding in Reading. There were several water rescues from trapped vehicles on North 6th and North 8th Streets between Oley and Elm Streets. Basement flooding also occurred at Reading City Hall.  Event precipitation totals included 1.41 inches in Reading and 1.40 inches in Wyomissing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Whitehall Township. A vehicle became partially submerged at the intersection of Mickley Road and Pennsylvania State Route 145.  Event precipitation totals included 1.19 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow and moderate instability ahead of an approaching cold front helped to spawn showers and thunderstorms, some of which were severe, across the northern West Virginia pan-handle, and western Pennsylvania on the 7th. With dry air present aloft, several of the storms produced damaging wind gusts that brought down trees and power lines. Front slowed as it crossed into Pennsylvania early on the 8th, and with plenty of moisture, heavy rain became a threat overnight. Some flooding was reported in Allegheny county in Shaler and McCandless Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous large branches, with a diameter of up to 5 inches, covering the road and yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania on the 19th and 20th except for the Poconos.  High temperatures both days reached into the lower to mid 90s. The 19th was slightly hotter overall and had some of the highest heat indices of the summer. There were two heat related deaths in Philadelphia on the 20th: an 88 year-old female and 64-year-old male.  The morning low at Philadelphia International Airport on the 20th was 81 degrees. The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F in southeast Pennsylvania. A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in Eastern Pennsylvania. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone.  In Philadelphia, walking tours in the historic district were cancelled.  A 22-year-old man from Virginia drowned after jumping into the Delaware River in Bristol (Bucks County) on the 19th. | |Highest temperatures included 95 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, 94 degrees in Reading (Berks County), Horsham (Montgomery County) and Westtown (Chester County), 93 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Perkasie (Bucks County), Coatesville (Chester County) and Nazareth (Northampton County), 92 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Saint Davids (Delaware County), 91 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 90 degrees in Saylorsburg (Monroe County).

The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down hundreds and hundreds of trees and caused some structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four people were injured in Philadelphia.  Hardest hit were Philadelphia, Chester and in particular central and western Delaware County.  Delaware County declared a state of emergency and suffered an estimated five million dollars in property damage. Five people were injured. While there were multiple reports of funnel clouds, no confirmed tornadoes occurred. Numerous homes and vehicles were damaged, mainly by fallen trees. Many roads were impassable through the next day because of downed trees. SEPTA had to suspend all of its commuter regional rail service on the 23rd in the Philadelphia Metropolitan area because of debris on the tracks and downed wires. Full service was back on the 25th. Amtrak suspended both Northeast Corridor Service between Philadelphia and Washington D.C. and Keystone service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg. That service was restored on the 24th. PECO Energy reported about 250,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania: close to 100,000 in Delaware County, close 75,000 in Chester County and nearly 50,000 within Philadelphia. Mutual assistance repair crews from Ohio, Kentucky, Maryland, West Virginia and New York helped with repairs. At 6 a.m. EDT on the 24th, about 161,000 customers (half in Delaware County) were still without power. That number dropped to 121,000 on the evening of the 24th, 50,000 on the 25th and 16,000 on the 26th. Full restoration occurred on the 28th. In Chester County, Verizon Wireless cell phone service was also lost on the 23rd and the 24th. Commuters were stuck on a train after power was lost on the 23rd. County courts remained closed on the 24th. In Delaware County,  PPL Park was used to distribute ice, cell phone recharge and showers. County offices re-opened on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees in multiple areas within West Bradford Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approaching from the Great Lakes and a weak pre-frontal surface trough helped to trigger a few strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of the 4th. The area most affected by the thunderstorms extended from Berks County into the Lehigh Valley where damaging wind gusts, power outages, and a lightning strike to a structure were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike was reported to a home along Morningside Drive in Palmer Township. The lightning reportedly hit in the yard and ran up the gutter into the eve of the home's attic. No injuries were reported.

Front slowed as it crossed into Pennsylvania early on the 8th, and with plenty of moisture, heavy rain became a threat overnight. Some flooding was reported in Allegheny county in Shaler and McCandless Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 center reported trees down blocking Main Street in Summerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed in the warm sector of the Susquehanna Valley and moved through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and first half of the evening on the 9th. The line also spawned an EF-1 tornado in Berks County. During the evening the line of thunderstorms also caused flash flooding.  A warm front lifted northeast into the Lehigh Valley during the afternoon of the 9th. This permitted sufficient daytime heating preceding an approaching cold front. In addition, there was enough shear in the atmosphere for a tornado to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado occurred in Tilden Township in Berks County and injured one person.  The tornado touched down near Stone Road and moved eastnortheast, passing through the Blue Mountain Academy before crossing Mountain Road into a campground area before lifting.  The most significant damage occurred to the Blue Mountain Elementary School. The roof of the school was completely removed by the tornado. The building is a total loss.  The 52-year-old principal inside the school at the time of the tornado was injured and was found covered by ceiling tiles.  Pieces of the roof were found hundreds of yards away in trees.  Hardwood trees were snapped along the tornado's path with a soybean field having debris dragged through it with visible arcing marks.  ||The estimated path length of the EF-1 tornado was three-quarters of a mile with a maximum path width of 75 yards.  The estimated maximum wind speed was 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania on the 19th and 20th except for the Poconos.  High temperatures both days reached into the lower to mid 90s. The 19th was slightly hotter overall and had some of the highest heat indices of the summer. There were two heat related deaths in Philadelphia on the 20th: an 88 year-old female and 64-year-old male.  The morning low at Philadelphia International Airport on the 20th was 81 degrees. The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F in southeast Pennsylvania. A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in Eastern Pennsylvania. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone.  In Philadelphia, walking tours in the historic district were cancelled.  A 22-year-old man from Virginia drowned after jumping into the Delaware River in Bristol (Bucks County) on the 19th. | |Highest temperatures included 95 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, 94 degrees in Reading (Berks County), Horsham (Montgomery County) and Westtown (Chester County), 93 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Perkasie (Bucks County), Coatesville (Chester County) and Nazareth (Northampton County), 92 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Saint Davids (Delaware County), 91 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 90 degrees in Saylorsburg (Monroe County).

The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down hundreds and hundreds of trees and caused some structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four people were injured in Philadelphia.  Hardest hit were Philadelphia, Chester and in particular central and western Delaware County.  Delaware County declared a state of emergency and suffered an estimated five million dollars in property damage. Five people were injured. While there were multiple reports of funnel clouds, no confirmed tornadoes occurred. Numerous homes and vehicles were damaged, mainly by fallen trees. Many roads were impassable through the next day because of downed trees. SEPTA had to suspend all of its commuter regional rail service on the 23rd in the Philadelphia Metropolitan area because of debris on the tracks and downed wires. Full service was back on the 25th. Amtrak suspended both Northeast Corridor Service between Philadelphia and Washington D.C. and Keystone service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg. That service was restored on the 24th. PECO Energy reported about 250,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania: close to 100,000 in Delaware County, close 75,000 in Chester County and nearly 50,000 within Philadelphia. Mutual assistance repair crews from Ohio, Kentucky, Maryland, West Virginia and New York helped with repairs. At 6 a.m. EDT on the 24th, about 161,000 customers (half in Delaware County) were still without power. That number dropped to 121,000 on the evening of the 24th, 50,000 on the 25th and 16,000 on the 26th. Full restoration occurred on the 28th. In Chester County, Verizon Wireless cell phone service was also lost on the 23rd and the 24th. Commuters were stuck on a train after power was lost on the 23rd. County courts remained closed on the 24th. In Delaware County,  PPL Park was used to distribute ice, cell phone recharge and showers. County offices re-opened on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down over 100 trees across Upland Borough and numerous roadways were closed and blocked by fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms that formed in the warm sector of the Susquehanna Valley and moved through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and first half of the evening on the 9th. It produced wind damage along its leading edge and then some flash flooding during the evening of the 9th in extreme southeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in lower Montgomery County mainly in Lower and Upper Merion Townships. Vehicles were stuck in flash flood waters on North Gulph Road, City Avenue and Haverford Avenue.  Event precipitation totals included 1.52 inches in Blue Bell, most of which fell in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of severe thunderstorms to move through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Estimated wind gusts as high as around 85 mph knocked down hundreds and hundreds of trees and caused some structural damage to homes and vehicles, mainly from fallen trees. Four people were injured in Philadelphia.  Hardest hit were Philadelphia, Chester and in particular central and western Delaware County.  Delaware County declared a state of emergency and suffered an estimated five million dollars in property damage. Five people were injured. While there were multiple reports of funnel clouds, no confirmed tornadoes occurred. Numerous homes and vehicles were damaged, mainly by fallen trees. Many roads were impassable through the next day because of downed trees. SEPTA had to suspend all of its commuter regional rail service on the 23rd in the Philadelphia Metropolitan area because of debris on the tracks and downed wires. Full service was back on the 25th. Amtrak suspended both Northeast Corridor Service between Philadelphia and Washington D.C. and Keystone service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg. That service was restored on the 24th. PECO Energy reported about 250,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania: close to 100,000 in Delaware County, close 75,000 in Chester County and nearly 50,000 within Philadelphia. Mutual assistance repair crews from Ohio, Kentucky, Maryland, West Virginia and New York helped with repairs. At 6 a.m. EDT on the 24th, about 161,000 customers (half in Delaware County) were still without power. That number dropped to 121,000 on the evening of the 24th, 50,000 on the 25th and 16,000 on the 26th. Full restoration occurred on the 28th. In Chester County, Verizon Wireless cell phone service was also lost on the 23rd and the 24th. Commuters were stuck on a train after power was lost on the 23rd. County courts remained closed on the 24th. In Delaware County,  PPL Park was used to distribute ice, cell phone recharge and showers. County offices re-opened on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Borough of Brookhaven was also hit very hard by the squall line of severe thunderstorms. Dozens and dozens of trees were knocked down across the borough and the entire borough lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed in the warm sector of the Susquehanna Valley and moved through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and first half of the evening on the 9th. In addition, there was enough shear in the atmosphere for a tornado to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a very large tree within Washington Square Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds, large hail and a tornado affected Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the afternoon into the early evening on the 30th. Hardest hit were the Lehigh Valley which experienced a strong microburst and Chester County which experienced a tornado. A state of emergency was declared in Whitehall Township (Lehigh County).  Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 11,000 of its customers lost power in the Lehigh Valley. Hardest hit by power outages included Whitehall and Catasauqua in Lehigh County and Hanover Township east through Lower Nazareth Township in Northampton County.  First Energy reported 4,000 of its customers lost power in Northampton County. Power was not fully restored until the night of July 1st. Heavy rain also caused some flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos.  The most severe thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary that slowly lifted northeast on the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong microburst caused wind damage in Whitehall Township in and near the Stiles section of the township. Because of the wind damage, a township state of emergency was declared.  Numerous trees were knocked down and many roads were impassable due to the downed trees. The downed trees damaged twenty-nine homes and buildings. One building sustained major damage. The township advised residents to stay off the roads. Around 600 Pennsylvania, Power and Light customers within the township were still without power late in the evening on the 30th.  Some of the most significant crop damage occurred along Columbia Street where large sections of corn fields were flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The NWS confirmed an EF1 tornado just south of Mifflinburg on August 20, 2015. The tornado touched down just west of Route 304 and crossed a farm property just north of the village of Dice. A large shed was completely destroyed, and a barn had a portion of its roof torn off. The tornado passed eastward across Route 304 and dissipated. The path length was approximately a quarter of a mile, and the path width was around 100 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado was confirmed by an NWS survey team just south of Mifflinburg in Union County. A large shed was destroyed and a roof was partially torn off of a barn. Peak winds were estimated at 90 mph, classifying this as an EF1.

A stationary front provided the focus for concentrated thunderstorms across the Northern Tier counties in Pennsylvania. Water was a few feet deep on some village streets, and rushing flood waters also destroyed culverts on several area roads.

A 22-year-old man from Virginia drowned after jumping into the Delaware River in Bristol (Bucks County) on the 19th. | |Highest temperatures included 95 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, 94 degrees in Reading (Berks County), Horsham (Montgomery County) and Westtown (Chester County), 93 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Perkasie (Bucks County), Coatesville (Chester County) and Nazareth (Northampton County), 92 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Saint Davids (Delaware County), 91 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County) and 90 degrees in Saylorsburg (Monroe County).

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed in the warm sector of the Susquehanna Valley and moved through southeast Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and first half of the evening on the 9th. The line also spawned an EF-1 tornado in Berks County. During the evening the line of thunderstorms also caused flash flooding.  A warm front lifted northeast into the Lehigh Valley during the afternoon of the 9th. This permitted sufficient daytime heating preceding an approaching cold front. In addition, there was enough shear in the atmosphere for a tornado to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires that damaged five homes in Rehrersburg (Tulpehocken Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania on the 19th and 20th except for the Poconos.  High temperatures both days reached into the lower to mid 90s. The 19th was slightly hotter overall and had some of the highest heat indices of the summer. There were two heat related deaths in Philadelphia on the 20th: an 88 year-old female and 64-year-old male.  The morning low at Philadelphia International Airport on the 20th was 81 degrees. The combination of heat and humidity brought afternoon heat index values as high as 100F to 105F on the 19th. A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the region during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F in southeast Pennsylvania. A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in Eastern Pennsylvania. ||To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended. In Philadelphia, the Corporation for Aging had councilors and nurses on the phone.  In Philadelphia, walking tours in the historic district were cancelled. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of a weak cold front as it crossed the upper Ohio Valley. With weak upper level support, only a few storms became severe but with little steering flow, some storms produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 center reported trees down on route 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Double barrel cold fronts helped trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms across southeast Pennsylvania during the morning of the 15th. Thunderstorms that were accompanied by very heavy rain caused flash flooding in parts of southeastern Berks, northwest Montgomery and in Northampton County.  Actual rainfall amounts in these locations were as high as 2 to 4 inches, most of which fell during a three hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in southern Northampton County in the city of Bethlehem, Bethlehem Township and Palmer Township. In Bethlehem, East Market and Jenning Streets as well as East Boulevard were closed. In Bethlehem Township, a bus became stuck in flash flood waters. Stranded passengers were rescued from the bus. The Nancy Run Creek flooded within the township across Willow Park Road. The bridge over the creek at the intersection of Willow Park Road and Carter Road was flooded and closed.  In addition, Butztown Road was also closed. In Palmer Township, 25th Street was closed due to flash flooding and a couple of water rescues were performed. Event precipitation totals included 3.18 inches in Lower Nazareth Township and  2.29 inches in Nazareth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tore down trees and wires in several locations throughout Delaware County. This included one downed tree that snapped a utility pole in Media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, multiple thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and Poconos on the afternoon and evening of the 30th. The flash flooding in the Poconos persisted into July 1st. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached two inches in the Lehigh Valley and five inches in the Poconos.||The heavy rain on the 30th culminated a very wet month of June across Eastern Pennsylvania and Pennsylvania as a whole.  The June monthly precipitation total of 7.59 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was the 5th wettest June on record and the 8.88 June monthly precipitation total at the Philadelphia International Airport was the 3rd wettest June on record.  The June precipitation total of 6.22 inches in Reading (Berks County), was 2.44 inches wetter than normal and the June precipitation total of 8.77 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), was 4.39 inches wetter than normal. For the state of Pennsylvania, it was the second wettest June on record (8.20 inches) dating back to 1895.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brodhead Creek near Anamolink was above its 8 foot flood stage from 858 p.m. EDT through 1030 p.m. EDT on the 30th. It crested at 8.13 feet at 930 p.m. Pennsylvania State Route 447 which is located along the creek was flooded and remained closed into the overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front slowly moving north through Pennsylvania triggered widely scattered showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain mainly during the evening of the 6th. The thunderstorms with the heaviest rain affected central Delaware County and upper Bucks County. In the latter flash flooding occurred as Doppler Radar storm total estimates approached 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain called flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as flooding of roadways in Upper Bucks County from Perkasie east into Nockamixon and Tinicum Townships. Water was crossing roadways in Perkasie. One cooperative weather observer in Perkasie reported 2.05 inches of rain within two hours. Doppler Radar storm total estimates neared 4 inches farther to the east close to Lake Nockamixon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported trees down on Interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of a weak cold front as it crossed the upper Ohio Valley. With weak upper level support, only a few storms became severe but with little steering flow, some storms produced heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local 911 center reported trees blocking roads just east of Clintonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved east across southern Ontario on November 18th. The strong pressure gradient resulting from the low caused damaging down sloping southeast winds to develop across northern Erie County. Wind gusts of 60 mph or greater downed some trees and power lines in the City of Erie.  A peak gust of 51 mph was measured at Erie International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved east across southern Ontario on November 18th. The strong pressure gradient resulting from the low caused damaging down sloping southeast winds to develop across northern Erie County. Wind gusts of 60 mph or greater downed some trees and power lines in the City of Erie.  A peak gust of 51 mph was measured at Erie International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low passing over the region triggered moderate to heavy snow showers during the morning of the 3rd. This caused reduced visibilities, traveling difficulties, and some snow accumulation to around one inch, especially over the higher terrain through the Poconos. On the border of Carbon and Monroe Counties, a quick burst of snow resulted in 1.0 inch accumulation at Lake Harmony.

A seasonably cold westerly flow over the record-warm waters of Lake Erie produced localized lake-effect snowfall accumulation between 6 and 8 inches over far northwest Warren County. The heaviest snow bands with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour impacted the favored lake-effect snowbelt in northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observers in Bear Lake and Chandlers Valley reported 6 to 7 inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across Lake Erie during the afternoon of December 18th.  Lake effect snow showers developed that evening behind the front with a heavy west to east oriented band setting up across Ashtabula into Erie and Crawford Counties. The heaviest snow fell during the early morning hours of the 19th when snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour.  The snow showers finally tapered off during the afternoon hours of the 19th.  By the time the event was over much of inland Erie County and most of northern Crawford County had six or more inches of snow.  Peak snowfall totals included 15.5 inches at Edinboro in Erie County and 9.5 inches at Canadohta Lake in northern Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across Lake Erie during the afternoon of December 18th.  Lake effect snow showers developed that evening behind the front with a heavy west to east oriented band setting up across Ashtabula into Erie and Crawford Counties. The heaviest snow fell during the early morning hours of the 19th when snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour.  The snow showers finally tapered off during the afternoon hours of the 19th.  By the time the event was over much of inland Erie County and most of northern Crawford County had six or more inches of snow.  The peak snowfall total in Crawford County was 9.5 inches at Canadohta Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of frozen precipitation occurred from the evening of the 28th into the morning of the 29th over the higher terrain of the Poconos and also into portions of the Lehigh Valley. Some slippery conditions and traveling difficulties were reported in portions of Carbon, Monroe, Lehigh, and Northampton Counties as a result of a combination of sleet, freezing rain, and some snow that fell across the area before warmer air arrived and changed all precipitation over to rain by mid to late morning on the 29th. ||The mixture of frozen precipitation was caused in part by a low pressure system that tracked from the mid-Mississippi Valley on the afternoon of the 28th to the Great Lakes by the morning of the 29th. Its associated warm front lifted north toward Pennsylvania and gradually began to dislodge cold air at the surface that had been in place as a result of strong Canadian high pressure extending down across the northern Mid-Atlantic states. While some snow did occur, most of the frozen precipitation was in the form of sleet and then freezing rain as a warm layer of air expanded aloft and cold, sub-freezing air gradually eroded away at the surface. By mid to late morning on the 29th, surface temperatures were above freezing and precipitation had transitioned to rain. ||Storm total snow and sleet amounts included 1.1 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County); 0.8 inches in Stroudsburg, Mount Pocono, and Canadensis (Monroe County); 0.6 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County) and in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and also in Bangor (Northampton County); 0.5 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County); 0.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County); 0.2 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County); and a trace recorded at the Lehigh Valley International Airport (Lehigh County).||Storm total ice accretion as a result of freezing rain included 0.25 inches in Albrightsville and Summit Hill (Carbon County) and also in Cresco (Monroe County), 0.19 inches at the Pocono Mountains Municipal Airport (Monroe County), and a trace of freezing rain recorded in Stroudsburg (Monroe County). In addition to these measurements, there were also several reports of trees and wires down in Carbon and Monroe Counties where the sleet and freezing rain lasted the longest. A couple of trees were reported down in the following towns in Carbon County: Lansford, Jim Thorpe, Weatherly, and Nesquehoning. In addition, an electric powerline was reported down at the Penn Forest Reservoir in Carbon County, and one downed tree was reported in Tobyhanna (Monroe County).
